writing_type: UX product copy (in-app flows, errors, and recovery states)
user_goals_summary: Write product copy that reads as one coherent explanation across sequential screens, eliminates ambiguity at every step, and respects the user as a competent professional who wants to resolve the issue fast. Every piece of copy—error messages, recovery screens, CTAs—must earn its place by answering a specific question the user will have at that moment.

rubric.name: Information Continuity Across States
rubric.description: Sequential copy blocks (error → recovery → CTA) read as one continuous explanation, not disconnected notices. A user moving through the flow should feel like they're following a single narrative thread, not re-orienting at each screen.

dimensions.label: Copy follows a clear logic chain: what happened → impact on user → what to do → what the action achieves
Later screens build on information from earlier screens rather than restating or contradicting it
Detail increases as the user moves deeper into the flow (summary first, specifics later)
The full flow reads as though it could be one continuous paragraph if the screen breaks were removed

rubric.name: Diagnosis Before Direction
rubric.description: Copy explains what happened and why before telling the user what to do. The user should never have to ask 'but what actually happened?' before being presented with a fix.

dimensions.label: The cause of the issue appears before the call to action or recovery instruction
Copy names the responsible actor or system (e.g., 'your bank' not just 'something went wrong')
Every 'do this' instruction is preceded by a 'because this' explanation
Copy explicitly states the impact on the user (e.g., 'your card hasn't been charged') rather than leaving them to infer it

rubric.name: Outcome-Signaling CTAs
rubric.description: Buttons and action prompts describe the outcome of clicking, not just the task the user is about to perform. The user should know what the system will do as a result of their action.

dimensions.label: CTA text communicates what happens after the click, not just what the user is about to do
CTA accurately reflects the actual system behavior (e.g., auto-retry vs. manual confirm)
CTA implies resolution or progress, not just navigation to another screen
Primary CTA presents one unambiguous next step (secondary options are clearly subordinate)

rubric.name: Preemptive Clarity
rubric.description: Each copy block anticipates and answers the user's most likely next question at that moment. Copy should eliminate the gap between 'what does this say?' and 'what do I need to know?' so the user never stalls.

dimensions.label: The copy addresses the user's most immediate concern at this point in the flow (e.g., 'was I charged?')
Every state provides a clear next step — the user is never left without a path forward
No sentence leaves the user needing to guess at meaning or implications
Includes time-sensitive or state-sensitive context when it affects the user's decision (e.g., order hold duration)

rubric.name: Transactional Directness
rubric.description: Tone is clean, calm, and efficient — no emotional padding, no coddling, no drama. Copy respects the user as a competent professional who wants to resolve the issue and move on.

dimensions.label: No unnecessary reassurance phrases ('don't worry,' 'no need to panic,' 'let's fix this together')
Every word earns its place — no filler, hedging, or softening that doesn't add information
Tone is matter-of-fact and neutral, never urgent or anxiety-inducing
Copy assumes the user understands the domain and doesn't over-explain basic concepts

coaching_notes: This user treats copy as a system, not a collection of isolated strings — they evaluate whether the full flow works as a coherent explanation, not whether individual screens are 'good.' Their sharpest feedback targets ambiguity gaps: any moment where the user has to infer, guess, or mentally bridge between two pieces of information is treated as a failure. When drafting, lead with the strongest option matching their stated voice rather than offering stylistic variety — they know their product voice and don't need to explore.

rubric.description: Copy explains what happened and why before telling the user what to do. The user should never have to ask 'but what actually happened?' before being presented with a fix.

dimensions.label: The cause of the issue appears before the call to action or recovery instruction
Copy names the responsible actor or system (e.g., 'your bank' not just 'something went wrong')
Every 'do this' instruction is preceded by a 'because this' explanation
Copy explicitly states the impact on the user (e.g., 'your card hasn't been charged') rather than leaving them to infer it

rubric.description: Buttons and action prompts describe the outcome of clicking, not just the task the user is about to perform. The user should know what the system will do as a result of their action.

dimensions.label: CTA text communicates what happens after the click, not just what the user is about to do
CTA accurately reflects the actual system behavior (e.g., auto-retry vs. manual confirm)
CTA implies resolution or progress, not just navigation to another screen
Primary CTA presents one unambiguous next step (secondary options are clearly subordinate)

rubric.name: Outcome-Signaling CTAs
rubric.description: Buttons and action prompts describe the outcome of clicking, not just the task the user is about to perform. The user should know what the system will do as a result of their action.

dimensions.label: CTA text communicates what happens after the click, not just what the user is about to do
CTA accurately reflects the actual system behavior (e.g., auto-retry vs. manual confirm)
CTA implies resolution or progress, not just navigation to another screen
Primary CTA presents one unambiguous next step (secondary options are clearly subordinate)

rubric.name: Value-Forward Framing
rubric.description: Copy orients the user toward what becomes possible after they act, not just what is currently absent or broken. In empty states and onboarding moments especially, copy should make the product's value concrete and the next action feel worthwhile — not just describe the void.

dimensions.label: Copy communicates what the product will do for the user once they complete the action, not just what is currently missing
Empty or zero-data states are framed as a starting point, not a dead end or failure condition
The user can infer a concrete benefit from acting — not just that acting will fix an absence

rubric.name: Diagnosis Before Direction
rubric.description: Copy explains what happened and why before telling the user what to do. The user should never have to ask 'but what actually happened?' before being presented with a fix.

dimensions.label: The cause of the issue appears before the call to action or recovery instruction
Copy names the responsible actor or system (e.g., 'your bank' not just 'something went wrong')
Every 'do this' instruction is preceded by a 'because this' explanation
Copy explicitly states the impact on the user (e.g., 'your card hasn't been charged') rather than leaving them to infer it

rubric.name: Preemptive Clarity
rubric.description: Each copy block anticipates and answers the user's most likely next question at that moment — including both 'what is happening?' and 'what will this product do for me?' Copy should eliminate the gap between 'what does this say?' and 'what do I need to know and do next?' so the user never stalls.

dimensions.label: The copy addresses the user's most immediate concern at this point in the flow (e.g., 'was I charged?' or 'did my connection work?')
Every state provides a clear next step — the user is never left without a path forward
No sentence leaves the user needing to guess at meaning or implications
Includes time-sensitive or state-sensitive context when it affects the user's decision (e.g., order hold duration)
Copy confirms what the system has already done (e.g., 'your bank is connected') so the user isn't left wondering whether a prior step succeeded

rubric.name: Transactional Directness
rubric.description: Tone is clean, calm, and efficient — no emotional padding, no coddling, no drama. Copy respects the user as a competent professional who wants to resolve the issue and move on.

dimensions.label: No unnecessary reassurance phrases ('don't worry,' 'no need to panic,' 'let's fix this together')
Every word earns its place — no filler, hedging, or softening that doesn't add information
Tone is matter-of-fact and neutral, never urgent or anxiety-inducing
Copy assumes the user understands the domain and doesn't over-explain basic concepts

writing_type: UX product copy (in-app flows, errors, and recovery states)
user_goals_summary: Write product copy that reads as one coherent explanation across sequential screens, eliminates ambiguity at every step, and respects the user as a competent professional who wants to resolve the issue fast. Every piece of copy—error messages, recovery screens, CTAs—must earn its place by answering a specific question the user will have at that moment.
coaching_notes: This user treats copy as a system, not a collection of isolated strings — they evaluate whether the full flow works as a coherent explanation, not whether individual screens are 'good.' Their sharpest feedback targets ambiguity gaps: any moment where the user has to infer, guess, or mentally bridge between two pieces of information is treated as a failure. When drafting, lead with the strongest option matching their stated voice rather than offering stylistic variety — they know their product voice and don't need to explore.

rubric.name: Outcome-Signaling CTAs
rubric.description: Buttons and action prompts describe the outcome of clicking, not just the task the user is about to perform. The user should know what the system will do as a result of their action before they click.

dimensions.label: CTA text communicates what happens after the click, not just what the user is about to do
CTA accurately reflects the actual system behavior (e.g., auto-retry vs. manual confirm)
CTA implies resolution or progress, not just navigation to another screen
Primary CTA presents one unambiguous next step (secondary options are clearly subordinate)

No generic labels like 'Submit' or 'Sign up' — label reflects the specific outcome (e.g., 'Save changes', 'Start free trial'). In celebratory or milestone moments, prefer natural action verbs over precise-but-clinical ones — the label should feel appropriate to the emotional register of the state, not just technically accurate.

rubric.name: Diagnosis Before Direction
rubric.description: Error and failure states follow a strict order: what happened, why it happened, what the user should do next. Copy should never open with an instruction before establishing the cause. An error that only says 'Something went wrong' is a product failure.

dimensions.label: The cause of the issue appears before the call to action or recovery instruction
Copy names the responsible actor or system (e.g., 'your bank declined the charge' not 'something went wrong')
Every 'do this' instruction is preceded by a 'because this' explanation
Copy explicitly states the impact on the user (e.g., 'your card hasn't been charged') rather than leaving them to infer it
Tone in error states is calm and precise — not apologetic, not alarming

rubric.name: Specific Consequence Confirmations
rubric.description: Confirmation dialogs name the exact consequence of the action — including what will be affected, how many items, and whether it's reversible. Vague prompts like 'Are you sure?' train users to click through without reading and must never appear.

dimensions.label: Dialog names the specific object(s) affected (e.g., 'Delete 3 invoices' not 'Delete items')
Irreversibility is stated explicitly when the action cannot be undone
No vague prompts like 'Are you sure?' or 'Confirm action' — the consequence IS the question
Confirm button label matches the consequence (e.g., 'Delete invoices' not 'Yes' or 'OK')

rubric.name: Empty States as Entry Points
rubric.description: Empty and zero-data states guide the user toward their first meaningful action. They should feel like a helpful nudge, not a dead end. Copy communicates what the user will gain by acting, not just that nothing exists yet.

dimensions.label: Empty states are framed as a starting point, not a failure condition or absence
The user can infer a concrete benefit from acting — not just that acting will fix an absence
Empty state includes a clear, specific CTA that leads directly to the first action
Copy never uses dead-end phrases like 'Nothing here yet' or 'No data available' without a path forward

rubric.name: Context-Sensitive Tone
rubric.description: Tone shifts to match the emotional weight of the moment. Celebratory milestones (first successful payment, account connected) can be warm. Error states must be calm and precise. Routine actions should feel invisible — efficient, not cheerful or dramatic. Applying a single tone across all states feels robotic and erodes trust. In celebratory states, do not sacrifice conversational warmth for marginal gains in specificity — a label that feels slightly more 'exact' but sounds clinical is the wrong trade-off.

dimensions.label: Celebratory moments use warm, conversational language — prefer natural phrasing ('See your breakdown') over formal or dashboard-adjacent verbs ('View', 'Access') that undercut the tone
Error states are calm and factual — no unnecessary apology, no alarm
Routine confirmations and status messages are efficient and unobtrusive
No unnecessary reassurance phrases ('don't worry,' 'no need to panic,' 'let's fix this together') in non-celebratory states
Every word earns its place — no filler, hedging, or softening that doesn't add information
In milestone and success states, verb choices in CTAs and body copy should match the warmth of the moment — avoid swapping natural phrasing for formal alternatives solely in the name of precision

rubric.name: Preemptive Clarity
rubric.description: Each copy block anticipates and answers the user's most likely next question at that moment. Copy should eliminate the gap between 'what does this say?' and 'what do I need to know and do next?' so the user never stalls.

dimensions.label: The copy addresses the user's most immediate concern at this point in the flow (e.g., 'was I charged?' or 'did my connection work?')
Every state provides a clear next step — the user is never left without a path forward
No sentence leaves the user needing to guess at meaning or implications
Copy confirms what the system has already done (e.g., 'your bank is connected') so the user isn't left wondering whether a prior step succeeded
Includes time-sensitive or state-sensitive context when it affects the user's decision

writing_type: UX product copy (in-app flows, errors, and recovery states)
user_goals_summary: Write product copy that reads as one coherent explanation across sequential screens, eliminates ambiguity at every step, and respects the user as a competent professional who wants to resolve the issue fast. Every piece of copy—error messages, recovery screens, CTAs—must earn its place by answering a specific question the user will have at that moment.
coaching_notes: This user treats copy as a system, not a collection of isolated strings — they evaluate whether the full flow works as a coherent explanation, not whether individual screens are 'good.' Their sharpest feedback targets ambiguity gaps: any moment where the user has to infer, guess, or mentally bridge between two pieces of information is treated as a failure. When drafting, lead with the strongest option matching their stated voice rather than offering stylistic variety — they know their product voice and don't need to explore.

writing_type: UX product copy (in-product microcopy: empty states, error flows, CTAs, onboarding moments)
user_goals_summary: This user writes UX copy for B2B/prosumer fintech products where users are competent professionals who want clarity and efficiency. They prioritize copy that makes the user the agent (not the system), signals outcomes rather than labeling tasks, and calibrates emotional register to the weight of the moment. Each copy element in a set must have a distinct, non-overlapping job, and flows should read as connected narratives rather than isolated blocks.

rubric.name: Outcome-Signaling CTAs
rubric.description: Buttons and action prompts describe what changes when the user clicks, not just the task being performed. The user should be able to predict what happens after clicking from the label alone. A CTA that names a gesture without naming the consequence leaves the user guessing.

dimensions.label: CTA names the outcome or consequence of clicking, not just the action the user performs
User can predict what happens after clicking from the label alone, without relying on surrounding context
No generic labels like 'Submit,' 'Continue,' or 'Get Started' — label reflects the specific outcome
Verb describes what the user experiences, not what the system does internally (e.g., 'send' not 'create' when the user experiences sending)
CTA sits in the same emotional register as the surrounding copy — doesn't drop into menu-item or system-command tone when the context is warmer

rubric.name: User-as-Agent Voice
rubric.description: Copy is written from the user's perspective, not the system's. Verbs should feel like the user's actions, not the software's internal operations. The product should sound like a knowledgeable peer, not a system issuing commands or a marketing department making promises to already-converted users.

dimensions.label: Verbs describe what the user does or experiences, not what the system does on the back end
Copy avoids marketing language inside the product — no benefit pitches or value props aimed at users who have already committed
Language reads as a peer nudging or guiding, not a system issuing commands
Terminology matches what users call things in their own work, not internal product or engineering vocabulary

rubric.name: Diagnosis Before Direction
rubric.description: Error and failure states follow a strict order: what happened, why it happened, what the user should do next. Copy never opens with an instruction before establishing the cause. An error that only says 'Something went wrong' is a product failure, not just a copy failure.

dimensions.label: Error copy names what happened and why before suggesting a fix or next step
Copy names the responsible actor or system (e.g., 'your bank declined the charge' not 'something went wrong')
Tone in error states is calm and precise — not apologetic, not alarming, not dramatic
Error message and recovery screen read as one continuous explanation — user never feels they're reading two separate, disconnected notices
Copy anticipates and answers the user's most likely immediate question (e.g., 'was I charged?') before they have to ask it

rubric.name: Value Before Mechanics
rubric.description: Copy lands the payoff — what the user is about to gain or experience — before explaining how the feature works. Description copy should make the result feel real and close, not narrate the product's operations or list feature specifications.

dimensions.label: Copy leads with what the user gets or experiences, not what the feature does
Payoff is framed in terms the user cares about, not feature specifications or system capabilities
Technical details and mechanics follow the value statement, not the other way around
Copy does not describe absence or narrate the void (e.g., 'Nothing here yet') — it points toward what's coming

rubric.name: Moment-Matched Tone
rubric.description: Tone shifts to match the emotional weight of the moment. Milestone and first-time moments can carry warmth and acknowledgment. Error states must be calm and precise. Routine actions should feel invisible — efficient, not cheerful or dramatic. Applying a single register across all states feels robotic and erodes trust.

dimensions.label: Milestone and first-time moments acknowledge the user's effort or progress before pointing forward (e.g., 'The hard part's done')
Error states are composed and diagnostic — no drama, no coddling, no false cheerfulness
Routine interactions are efficient and don't carry unnecessary emotional weight or enthusiasm
Warmth is conveyed through natural, conversational phrasing — not exclamation points, emoji, or effusive language
Copy within a single set shifts register between setup and payoff — the emotional temperature is not flat throughout

rubric.name: Distinct Roles Per Copy Element
rubric.description: Each piece of copy in a set (headline, description, CTA) has a clear, non-overlapping job. No element duplicates another's work. Headlines orient, descriptions bridge context and mood, CTAs carry the action. Together they read as a connected arc, not separate notices.

dimensions.label: Headlines orient the user to where they are or what just happened — they don't sell, tease, or preview the action
Subtext or description sets context and mood without restating the headline or previewing the CTA
CTA carries the action forward without restating the headline or description
No element is doing work that belongs to another element — information appears once, in the right place
Copy blocks in a flow read as a connected narrative, not isolated pieces of information

coaching_notes: This user iterates extensively on CTA labels — they care deeply about whether buttons describe outcomes vs. actions and will reopen 'locked' decisions repeatedly. This is their core craft concern; patience and precision matter here. They draw a sharp line between marketing copy and product copy — benefit pitches and value props feel dishonest once a user is inside the product. Warmth should be calibrated to the moment: absent in routine interactions, quietly present at milestones ('The hard part's done. Now comes the good part.'), and never performative.

rubric.description: Error and failure states follow a strict order: what happened, why it happened, what the user should do next. Copy never opens with an instruction before establishing the cause. An error that only says 'Something went wrong' is a product failure, not just a copy failure.

dimensions.label: Error copy names what happened and why before suggesting a fix or next step
Copy names the responsible actor or system (e.g., 'your bank declined the charge' not 'something went wrong')
Tone in error states is calm and precise — not apologetic, not alarming, not dramatic
Error message and recovery screen read as one continuous explanation — user never feels they're reading two separate, disconnected notices
Copy anticipates and answers the user's most likely immediate question (e.g., 'was I charged?') before they have to ask it

dimensions.label: CTA names the outcome or consequence of clicking, not just the action the user performs
User can predict what happens after clicking from the label alone, without relying on surrounding context
No generic labels like 'Submit,' 'Continue,' or 'Get Started' — label reflects the specific outcome

CTA sits in the same emotional register as the surrounding copy — doesn't drop into menu-item or system-command tone when the context is warmer

dimensions.label: Dialog names the specific object(s) affected (e.g., '3 invoices' not 'these items')
Copy states whether the action is permanent or reversible
No vague prompts like 'Are you sure?' or 'Confirm action' — the consequence is the prompt
Destructive actions use direct, unambiguous language without being alarming

rubric.name: Empty States as Entry Points
rubric.description: Empty states guide the user toward their first meaningful action — they are not dead ends. Copy should feel like a helpful nudge from a knowledgeable peer, not a system reporting an absence. Every empty state should leave the user knowing exactly what to do next.

dimensions.label: Empty state includes a clear, specific call to action — not just a description of what's missing
Copy explains what the user will be able to do once they take the first action, not just that nothing exists yet
Tone feels like a nudge, not a dead end — no 'Nothing here yet' or 'No results found' without guidance

rubric.name: Moment-Matched Tone
rubric.description: Tone shifts to match the emotional weight of the moment. Milestone and first-time moments can carry warmth and acknowledgment. Error states must be calm and precise. Routine actions should feel invisible — efficient, not cheerful or dramatic. Applying a single register across all states feels robotic and erodes trust.

dimensions.label: Milestone and first-time moments acknowledge the user's effort or progress before pointing forward
Error states are composed and diagnostic — no drama, no coddling, no false cheerfulness
Routine interactions are efficient and don't carry unnecessary emotional weight or enthusiasm
Warmth is conveyed through natural, conversational phrasing — not exclamation points, emoji, or effusive language

The product should sound like a knowledgeable peer, not a system issuing commands or a marketing department pitching to users who've already converted.

dimensions.label: Verbs describe what the user does or experiences, not what the system does on the back end
Copy avoids marketing language inside the product — no benefit pitches or value props aimed at users who have already committed
Language reads as a peer nudging or guiding, not a system issuing commands
Terminology matches what users call things in their own work, not internal product or engineering vocabulary
No passive voice in instructions — 'Enter your card number' not 'Your card number should be entered'

User can predict what happens after clicking from the label alone, without relying on surrounding context
No generic labels like 'Submit,' 'Continue,' or 'Get Started' — label reflects the specific outcome
Verb describes what the user experiences, not what the system does internally (e.g., 'Send invoice' not 'Create record')

rubric.name: Specific Consequences in Confirmations
rubric.description: Confirmation dialogs must name the exact consequence of the action — what will be affected, how many items, and whether it's reversible. Vague prompts like 'Are you sure?' train users to click through without reading. The user should be able to make an informed decision from the dialog copy alone.

dimensions.label: Dialog names the specific object(s) affected (e.g., '3 invoices' not 'these items')
Copy states whether the action is permanent or reversible
No vague prompts like 'Are you sure?' or 'Confirm action' — the consequence is the prompt
Destructive actions use direct, unambiguous language without being alarming

rubric.name: Scannable, Action-Oriented Structure
rubric.description: Every string should make the user's next step obvious at a glance. Copy earns its place by reducing cognitive load — not by filling space. If a user has to re-read a string to understand what to do next, it hasn't done its job.

dimensions.label: The user's next action is identifiable from the copy alone without reading surrounding UI

Copy is as short as it can be without losing meaning — words that address likely user anxiety or reduce cognitive friction count as meaningful and should be retained. Possessives that ground instructions in the user's own context (e.g., 'your preferences,' 'your account') are not filler and should not be cut for brevity alone.

Hierarchy is reflected in copy length — primary messages are shorter and more direct than secondary ones. Structural reordering to shorten the primary clause is only appropriate when the result reads naturally — restructuring that produces grammatically awkward or tonally off phrasing is not preferred over the original word order.
No marketing language inside the product — copy assumes the user has already committed
Copy that directly addresses a likely user concern or misread — even in non-error states — should be retained even if it adds length

rubric.name: Empty States as Entry Points
rubric.description: Empty states guide the user toward their first meaningful action — they are not dead ends. Copy should feel like a helpful nudge from a knowledgeable peer, not a system reporting an absence. Every empty state should leave the user knowing exactly what to do next.

dimensions.label: Empty state includes a clear, specific call to action — not just a description of what's missing
Copy explains what the user will be able to do once they take the first action, not just that nothing exists yet
Tone feels like a nudge, not a dead end — no 'Nothing here yet' or 'No results found' without guidance

rubric.name: Moment-Matched Tone
rubric.description: Tone shifts to match the emotional weight of the moment. Milestone and first-time moments can carry warmth and acknowledgment. Error states must be calm and precise. Routine actions should feel invisible — efficient, not cheerful or dramatic. Applying a single register across all states feels robotic and erodes trust.

dimensions.label: Milestone and first-time moments acknowledge the user's effort or progress before pointing forward
Error states are composed and diagnostic — no drama, no coddling, no false cheerfulness
Routine interactions are efficient and don't carry unnecessary emotional weight or enthusiasm
Warmth is conveyed through natural, conversational phrasing — not exclamation points, emoji, or effusive language

rubric.name: Consistent Terminology and Voice
rubric.description: The same concept is always called the same thing across a flow. Terminology matches what users call things in their own work, not internal product or engineering vocabulary. Instructions are always active voice — the user is the agent, not the recipient of a system's behavior. No placeholder text should ever reach production.

dimensions.label: The same object, action, or concept uses the same label everywhere in the same flow — no synonyms that introduce ambiguity
No passive voice in instructions — 'Enter your card number' not 'Your card number should be entered'
Terminology reflects how users refer to things in their own work, not internal or engineering vocabulary
No placeholder text (e.g., 'Lorem ipsum,' '[INSERT COPY],' 'TBD') present in any production-facing string
No clever wordplay or puns that could slow comprehension or read differently across user contexts

writing_type: UX product copy (in-product microcopy: empty states, error flows, CTAs, onboarding moments)
user_goals_summary: This user writes UX copy for B2B/prosumer fintech products where users are competent professionals who want clarity and efficiency. They prioritize copy that makes the user the agent (not the system), signals outcomes rather than labeling tasks, and calibrates emotional register to the weight of the moment. Each copy element in a set must have a distinct, non-overlapping job, and flows should read as connected narratives rather than isolated blocks.
coaching_notes: This user iterates extensively on CTA labels — they care deeply about whether buttons describe outcomes vs. actions and will reopen 'locked' decisions repeatedly. This is their core craft concern; patience and precision matter here. They draw a sharp line between marketing copy and product copy — benefit pitches and value props feel dishonest once a user is inside the product. Warmth should be calibrated to the moment: absent in routine interactions, quietly present at milestones ('The hard part's done. Now comes the good part.'), and never performative.

writing_type: Product/UX copy for B2B fintech tools
user_goals_summary: Writes UI copy for a fintech product used by small business owners and finance ops professionals. Values copy that sounds like a knowledgeable peer — calm, direct, and specific — where every element has one clear job, tone matches the emotional weight of the moment, and the user always knows what happens next without filling in gaps themselves.

rubric.name: Outcome-Signaling CTAs
rubric.description: Buttons and action prompts describe what changes when the user clicks, not just the task being performed. The user should be able to predict what happens after clicking from the label alone. A CTA that names a gesture without naming the consequence leaves the user guessing.

dimensions.label: CTA names the outcome or consequence of clicking, not just the action the user performs
User can predict what happens after clicking from the label alone, without relying on surrounding context
No generic labels like 'Submit,' 'Continue,' or 'Get Started' — label reflects the specific outcome
Verb describes what the user experiences, not what the system does internally (e.g., 'Send invoice' not 'Create record')
CTA sits in the same emotional register as the surrounding copy — doesn't drop into menu-item or system-command tone when the context is warmer

rubric.name: Diagnosis Before Direction
rubric.description: Error and failure states follow a strict order: what happened, why it happened, what the user should do next. Copy never opens with an instruction before establishing the cause. An error that only says 'Something went wrong' is a product failure, not just a copy failure.

dimensions.label: Error copy names what happened and why before suggesting a fix or next step
Copy names the responsible actor or system (e.g., 'your bank declined the charge' not 'something went wrong')
Tone in error states is calm and precise — not apologetic, not alarming, not dramatic
Copy anticipates and answers the user's most likely immediate question (e.g., 'was I charged?') before they have to ask it
Error message and recovery screen read as one continuous explanation — user never feels they are reading two separate, disconnected notices

rubric.name: Moment-Matched Tone
rubric.description: Tone shifts to match the emotional weight of the moment. Milestone and first-time moments can carry warmth and acknowledgment. Error states must be calm and precise. Routine actions should feel invisible. The product should always sound like a knowledgeable peer who paused before writing — never like a system confirming inputs or a corporation managing expectations.

dimensions.label: Milestone and first-time moments acknowledge the user's effort or progress before pointing forward
Error states are composed and diagnostic — no drama, no coddling, no false cheerfulness
Routine interactions are efficient and don't carry unnecessary emotional weight or enthusiasm
Warmth is conveyed through natural, plain phrasing — not exclamation points, emoji, or effusive language
Copy within a single set shifts register between setup and payoff — the emotional temperature is not flat throughout

rubric.name: Earned Specificity
rubric.description: Reassurance and value are earned through concrete, specific language — never through comfort phrases, abstract claims, or corporate filler. If the copy asserts a quality ('we take that seriously,' 'in good hands') instead of demonstrating it with a specific detail, it hasn't done its job. Say the true, plain thing.

dimensions.label: No worn-out comfort phrases — 'in good hands,' 'the attention it deserves,' 'we take that seriously' are replaced with concrete details
Copy demonstrates qualities through specific, verifiable details rather than asserting them abstractly
No abstract filler words like 'accordingly,' 'appropriately,' or 'as needed' — each word carries specific meaning
Descriptions favor what IS happening over what ISN'T — negative definition is used only when the contrast itself earns specificity

rubric.name: Coherent Copy Flow
rubric.description: Sequential copy elements (headline → body → CTA, error → recovery) read as one continuous explanation, not separate notices. Each element has a distinct job — headline orients, body bridges, CTA acts — and no element restates or previews another. The user never has to mentally stitch pieces together.

dimensions.label: Sequential copy blocks read as one continuous narrative — user never feels they're reading disconnected pieces
Each element has a distinct role — no element duplicates or previews the work of another
Earlier elements set up later ones — subtext earns the CTA rather than restating it
Copy that directly addresses a likely user concern or anxiety is retained even if it adds length — reassurance that does real work is not filler
No marketing or benefit language inside the product — copy assumes the user has already committed

rubric.name: Empty States as Entry Points
rubric.description: Empty and zero-data states guide the user toward their first meaningful action — they are not dead ends. Headlines orient the user to the space they're in rather than narrating absence. Copy feels like a nudge from someone who's done it before, not a system reporting that nothing exists yet.

dimensions.label: Empty state includes a clear, specific call to action — not just a description of what's missing
Copy communicates what the user will gain from acting, not just that nothing exists yet
Tone feels like a nudge, not a dead end — no 'Nothing here yet' or 'No results found' without guidance
Headline orients the user to the space rather than narrating absence or building marketing-style anticipation

rubric.name: Consistent Terminology and Voice
rubric.description: The same concept uses the same word everywhere in a flow — terminology drift erodes trust. Words match how users refer to things in their own work, not internal or engineering vocabulary. Instructions use active voice with the user as agent. Possessives that ground instructions in the user's context are meaningful and should not be cut for brevity.

dimensions.label: The same object, action, or concept uses the same label everywhere in the same flow — no synonyms that introduce ambiguity
No passive voice in instructions — 'Enter your card number' not 'Your card number should be entered'
Terminology reflects how users refer to things in their own work, not internal or engineering vocabulary
Possessives that ground instructions in the user's own context (e.g., 'your preferences,' 'your documents') are retained — they are not filler

coaching_notes: Non-negotiables: error states must diagnose before directing, CTAs must never be generic labels, and no marketing language belongs inside the product. This user iterates at the word level and can identify sub-word tonal differences — they'll notice when a verb describes the system's operation vs. the user's experience. They prefer warmth earned through plainness ('The hard part's done') over warmth asserted through comfort phrases ('You're in good hands'). When iteration loops, they respond well to honest pushback about the limits of what copy can do — they respect being told 'this feeling belongs in the confirmation state, not the button' and will lock decisions when the constraint is named clearly.
